Colchicine to Suppress Pro-Tumorigenic Inflammation in Advanced Solid Tumors and Resected High-Risk Urothelial Carcinoma: A Pilot Trial

Clinical Trial Results (The Oncologist CTR format)

Alexander B. Karol; Che-Kai Tsao, MD; Himanshu Joshi, PhD; Deborah Y. Doroshow, MD, PhD; Matthew D. Galsky, MD; Thomas Marron, MD, PhD

July 23, 2026

Clinical Trial Results

Protocol: STUDY-21-001175 (IND 156493), version 3.0 (22 May 2023)
Institution: Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai / Mount Sinai Hospital
Principal investigator: Deborah Y. Doroshow, MD, PhD
Data cut: 23 July 2026 (REDCap export ProtocolNo2101175PRM_DATA_2026-05-18_2135.csv)

Abstract

Background: Pro-tumorigenic inflammation in advanced solid tumors is linked to IL-1β–NLRP3 signaling and elevated C-reactive protein (CRP). We conducted a single-center open-label pilot trial to evaluate colchicine effects on peripheral blood CRP in patients with advanced or recurrent solid tumors.

Methods: Eligible patients had metastatic or recurrent solid tumors with CRP greater than 5 mg/L and received colchicine 0.6 mg orally twice daily (Cohort 1 low dose). A protocol amendment limited later enrollments to a fixed 2-week course; earlier enrollments received indefinite colchicine with serial imaging. The primary endpoint was maximum percent CRP decline from cycle 1 day 1 to the lowest on-treatment value. Planned high-dose and adjuvant cohorts did not accrue.

Results: 16 patients consented; 11 enrolled and 5 screen-failed. 7 were treated under the original schedule and 4 under the amended schedule. 10 patients were evaluable for the primary endpoint. Mean maximum CRP decline was 19.2% (SD 30.0%); one-sided one-sample t-test p = 0.037, not meeting prespecified α = 0.025. Treatment-related adverse events occurred in 5 of 11 patients (45.5%); all were grade 1–2, most commonly diarrhea (4/11, 36.4%).

Conclusion: Colchicine 0.6 mg twice daily was tolerable with modest CRP suppression but did not meet the prespecified primary endpoint. The data do not support colchicine as a re-sensitization strategy in this setting. Trial registration: ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05279690.

Key words: colchicine; C-reactive protein; inflammation; solid tumors; pilot trial

Lessons Learned

  • Key outcome: The prespecified primary CRP endpoint was not met (one-sided p = 0.037 vs α = 0.025). With only 10 of 15 planned evaluable patients, the trial was underpowered to confirm the observed 19.2% mean CRP decline at the prespecified significance threshold.
  • Key lesson: Short-course low-dose colchicine was tolerable but yielded only a modest pharmacodynamic signal; future inflammation-modulation pilots in advanced solid tumors will likely need narrower disease selection, less restrictive eligibility, or larger cohorts to draw definitive conclusions.
  • Key reason: Accrual lagged because eligible patients were difficult to identify in routine oncology clinics (CRP greater than 5 mg/L plus organ-function requirements), 5 of 16 consented patients (31.2%) screen-failed before treatment (including 2 patient withdrawals, 1 organ dysfunction, and 1 acute clinical deterioration), and the adjuvant urothelial carcinoma cohort closed with zero enrollments.

Trial Information

Table 1. Trial information.
Trial information Description
Disease Advanced/recurrent solid tumors (Cohort 1); resected high-risk urothelial carcinoma planned for adjuvant nivolumab (Cohort 2, closed to accrual)
Stage of disease / treatment Metastatic/recurrent solid tumors (Cohort 1); post-surgical high-risk urothelial carcinoma (Cohort 2, not enrolled)
Prior therapy Variable prior systemic and local therapy per disease (see Patient Characteristics)
Type of study Single-center, open-label, non-randomized pilot
Primary endpoint Maximum percentage decline in peripheral blood CRP from cycle 1 day 1 during on-study colchicine
Secondary endpoints CTCAE v5.0 safety; PFS (Cohort 1); DFS/OS (Cohort 2); exploratory cytokines, ctDNA, tissue correlatives
Additional details Cohort 1 low-dose (BID) and high-dose (TID) were sequential; only low-dose enrolled. Cohort 2 closed for poor accrual before enrollment. During conduct, the protocol was amended: the first 7 enrolled patients received indefinite on-study colchicine with serial imaging surveillance; the subsequent 4 enrolled patients received a fixed 2-week colchicine course (phase 0 pharmacodynamic design). Exploratory Kaplan–Meier PFS/OS analyses were restricted to the original-schedule cohort (n = 7).

Drug Information

Table 2. Drug information.
Drug information Details
Generic/working name Colchicine
Drug type Anti-inflammatory alkaloid
Drug class Microtubule polymerization inhibitor (indirect NLRP3 pathway modulation)
Dose (Cohort 1 low) 0.6 mg oral BID × 14 days
Dose (Cohort 1 high) 0.6 mg oral TID × 14 days (not enrolled)
Dose (Cohort 2) 0.6 mg oral BID × 28 days with nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (cohort closed)
Route Oral
Schedule One cycle per protocol; missed doses not doubled

Primary Assessment Method

Table 3. Primary assessment method.
Primary assessment Value
Title Maximum percent decline in CRP from baseline (cycle 1 day 1) during post-baseline C1 on-treatment visits
Number of patients who signed informed consent 16
Number of patients enrolled 11
Number of screen failures before enrollment 5
Number evaluable for toxicity 11
Number evaluable for primary efficacy (CRP) 10
Evaluation method Peripheral blood CRP (mg/L) at protocol C1 visits; baseline = cycle 1 day 1 (or screening fallback if missing); minimum post-baseline value at C1D8/C1D15 used for maximum decline. Cycle 2 values excluded from primary endpoint per protocol treatment window.
Primary analysis result (Cohort 1 low dose) Mean max decline 19.2% (SD 30.0%); one-sided one-sample t-test vs 0, t = 2.02, p = 0.037 (prespecified α = 0.025)

Accrual Disposition

All 16 subjects who signed informed consent underwent screening evaluation. Enrollment status was recorded on the REDCap screening form (was_the_subject_enrolled: 1 = enrolled, 0 = screen failure). During trial conduct, a protocol amendment limited on-study colchicine to a fixed 2-week course for patients screened under REDCap Arm 2; patients screened under Arm 1 received the original indefinite on-study schedule with serial imaging surveillance. 7 enrolled patients are Kaplan–Meier–evaluable under the original schedule; 4 enrolled after the amendment contributed to toxicity and primary CRP pharmacodynamic analyses but were excluded from Kaplan–Meier PFS/OS estimates. When organ-function or CRP thresholds were not met, the report states the clinical failure (e.g., inadequate organ function) and the screening laboratory value when available. REDCap may classify these as “inclusion criteria” screen failures because adequate organ function and CRP elevation are protocol inclusion requirements.

Before original schedule 3 3 (18.8% of consented)

Figure 1. Maximum percent decline in C-reactive protein (CRP) from baseline during on-treatment assessment by enrolled patient (Cohort 1 low dose). Abbreviations: CRP, C-reactive protein.

Figure 2. Best percent change in target-lesion sum of diameters during continued colchicine treatment. The waterfall plot shows the best percent change from baseline among five patients treated on the original schedule; the accompanying table summarizes scan timing, CRP change near the best-response scan, and prior immunotherapy exposure. One patient, ID 4, experienced tumor shrinkage of 19% after prior ipilimumab plus nivolumab, although CRP increased near the time of the scan. This post hoc analysis is hypothesis-generating only. Abbreviations: CRP, C-reactive protein; IO, immunotherapy; SOD, sum of diameters.

Figure 3. Longitudinal peripheral blood CRP (mg/L) across protocol C1 visits (baseline C1D1 and post-baseline C1D8/C1D15) for enrolled patients with serial C1 values. Abbreviations: CRP, C-reactive protein.

Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics

Pharmacokinetics were not evaluated. Pharmacodynamic assessments included CRP (primary), ferritin, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) when collected per protocol.

Patient Status and Survival

Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were not captured on the dedicated REDCap PFS/OS form in this export (all subject_progression_status fields blank). Endpoints below were derived per patient from: (1) RECIST 1.1 best overall response (progressive disease [PD] date); (2) protocol therapy discontinuation for documented progression; (3) final subject status and date of death or last contact. Time zero = first investigational product dispense date, or enrollment if missing.

Analyses in this section include only the 7 patients enrolled under the original indefinite colchicine schedule with serial imaging surveillance (REDCap Arm 1). The 4 patients enrolled after the protocol amendment (fixed 2-week intervention; REDCap Arm 2) lacked the same longitudinal imaging framework and are reported for CRP pharmacodynamics and safety only.

Table 13. Exploratory best overall response (RECIST 1.1) among original-schedule enrolled patients (Kaplan–Meier cohort).

PD 2 2 (28.6%)

Figure 4. Progression-free survival (Kaplan–Meier) from treatment start among patients enrolled under the original indefinite colchicine schedule with surveillance imaging (n = 7; exploratory; not powered). PFS events: RECIST PD or discontinuation for progression. Abbreviations: PFS, progression-free survival; PD, progressive disease; RECIST, Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors.

Figure 5. Overall survival (Kaplan–Meier) from treatment start among patients enrolled under the original indefinite colchicine schedule with surveillance imaging (n = 7; exploratory; not powered). Abbreviations: OS, overall survival.

These survival analyses are descriptive and underpowered (Kaplan–Meier cohort n = 7; total enrolled n = 11). Medians and curves should be interpreted cautiously; several patients lack formal final-status forms but have tumor assessment or visit dates used for censoring. Patients enrolled after the protocol amendment (n = 4) are excluded because they received a fixed 2-week intervention without the same longitudinal imaging surveillance framework.

Statistical Analysis

Analyses followed protocol v3.0 (Section 12). The primary endpoint was tested with a one-sided one-sample t-test (H0: mean maximum CRP decline = 0) at α = 0.025 per the Bonferroni-adjusted Cohort 1 low-dose analysis plan. Exploratory progression-free and overall survival estimates used the Kaplan–Meier method (survival package in R) among the 7 patients enrolled under the original indefinite-treatment schedule with serial imaging surveillance (pre-amendment enrollments only). PFS and OS were derived from RECIST 1.1 assessments, protocol therapy discontinuation for progression, and final subject status because dedicated PFS/OS case report forms were incomplete in this export. Treatment-related adverse events required Possible, Probable, or Definite attribution to colchicine and were summarized by CTCAE v5.0 preferred term and worst grade per patient. Primary and exploratory analyses were performed in R per protocol v3.0 Section 12. Annotated R code and primary output are provided in the statistical analysis file.

Discussion

This single-center pilot (STUDY-21-001175) evaluated whether short-course low-dose colchicine could suppress peripheral blood CRP in patients with advanced or recurrent solid tumors. The trial closed under planned accrual: Cohort 1 low dose enrolled 11 patients, the high-dose sequential arm never opened, and Cohort 2 (resected high-risk urothelial carcinoma planned for adjuvant nivolumab) closed before enrollment because of poor accrual.

Among 16 consenting patients, 5 (31.2%) were screen failures before enrollment. 10 of 11 enrolled patients were evaluable for the primary endpoint. The mean maximum CRP decline was 19.2% (SD 30.0%). The prespecified one-sided one-sample t-test yielded p = 0.037, which does not meet the protocol’s multiplicity-adjusted α = 0.025 threshold.

With 10 evaluable patients instead of the planned 15 per low-dose group, post-hoc power to detect the observed effect (Cohen’s d ≈ 0.64) at α = 0.025 was approximately 44%.

Colchicine 0.6 mg twice daily for 14 days was well tolerated with grade 1–2 treatment-related adverse events only. Exploratory RECIST and Kaplan–Meier analyses were underpowered. These findings provide preliminary biologic proof-of-concept for inflammatory modulation but do not support colchicine as an effective re-sensitization strategy.
